content:
11 min read
Just now

--

A short story about how I stopped maintaining types/interfaces, data fetchers, and validation by hand. The cast: Hey API as the engine, with Zod and TanStack Query riding along as plugins.
If your backend has or can produce an OpenAPI spec, most of your frontend data layer is already written, you just haven’t generated it yet!
The slow leak
I have touched or developed some codebases that have the same quiet problem:

• An api/ folder full of hand-written clients,

• A types/ folder that almost matches the backend,

• And weirdly, a Post interface defined and extended in three or multiple places with slightly different fields because someone renamed body once and didn't catch every copy.
Nothing is on fire, and that’s the trap. The API layer drifts a little with every change until an undefined reaches production and you spend an afternoon proving the backend was right all along. The root cause is always the same, there's no single source of truth. The backend has one in its OpenAPI spec; the frontend keeps a parallel, hand-maintained universe right next to it.

The tool I kind of outgrew
For a while, my answer to this was swagger-typescript-api. You point it at a spec, get types, and typed functions back, genuinely useful, and I'd take it over writing clients by hand any day!
But it solved exactly one slice of the problem and then stopped. It gave me types and a client, and that was the whole offer. There was no runtime validation, so I was still trusting the backend blindly at the edge of the app. There were no React Query hooks, so I hand-wrote query keys and useQuery wrappers around every generated call. And the part that eventually pushed me to look elsewhere there was no real way to extend it. If I wanted validation schemas generated from the same spec, I was on my own. Always hectic, writing zod validation schemas, extending them e.t.c.
That’s the gap I kept hitting: a generator that produces code but isn’t built to grow new capabilities. I wanted one spec and one run to give me the client, the validation, and the data-fetching layer, not three separate chores stitched together by hand.

The spec already describes everything
Here’s the thing I’d been ignoring: the OpenAPI spec isn’t documentation you write after the fact, it’s a precise description of the contract, formats and all. I’ll keep the API deliberately tiny one resource, two endpoints because the point isn’t the size of the spec, it’s what tooling can do with it.
Sometimes you find yourself generating the schema after backend implementation, example when using frameworks like django with django rest framework coupled with a library like drf-spectacular to automatically generate OpenAPI 3 schemas, it might slightly change the workflow a little bit, but not in terms of what we deal with on frontend!
Here is the schema example we to use for now assuming it is already here!:
openapi: 3.1.0info:
title: Posts API # shows up in generated docs and client metadata
version: 1.0.0
paths:
/posts/{id}:
get:
operationId: getPostById # becomes the SDK function name: getPostById()
summary: Fetch a single post by its id
parameters:
- name: id
in: path
required: true
description: The post's unique identifier
schema:
type: string
format: uuid
responses:
'200':
description: The requested post
content:
application/json:
schema:
$ref: '#/components/schemas/Post' # response is a full Post
'404':
description: No post exists with that id
/posts:
post:
operationId: createPost # becomes createPost()
summary: Create a new post
requestBody:
required: true
content:
application/json:
schema:
$ref: '#/components/schemas/CreatePost' # request body, not a full Post
responses:
'201':
description: The post that was created
content:
application/json:
schema:
$ref: '#/components/schemas/Post'
components:
schemas:
# The full resource - what the server returns.
Post:
type: object
required: [id, title, body, publishedAt]
properties:
id:
type: string
format: uuid # the server owns this - clients never send it
title:
type: string
body:
type: string
publishedAt:
type: string
format: date-time # ISO timestamp, set by the server
# The create payload - what the client sends.
# Note it omits id and publishedAt: those are the server's to assign.
CreatePost:
type: object
required: [title, body]
properties:
title:
type: string
body:
type: string
Look at the format fields. uuid, date-time, that isn't noise, it's information most hand-written types quietly throw away. And notice the two schemas: Post is the full resource the server returns, while CreatePost is the leaner payload a client sends. That split is encoded right here in the spec, hold onto it, because it's exactly where Zod gets interesting in a moment.

Hey API is the engine & everything else is a plugin
Hey API is the piece that was missing for me, and the mental model is the whole point: Hey API is the generator; the SDK, Zod, and TanStack Query outputs are plugins. You’re not adopting four tools, instead, you’re adopting one engine and switching capabilities on as you need them. That plugin architecture is precisely what swagger-typescript-api never gave me.
You install it and describe what you want in a single config:
// openapi-ts.config.ts
import { defineConfig } from '@hey-api/openapi-ts';export default defineConfig({
// input: './openapi.json', // a local file or a live URL
input: './schema.yml', // for ourcase .yml file
output: 'src/client',
plugins: [
'@hey-api/sdk', // the typed client
'zod', // runtime validation schemas
'@tanstack/react-query', // hooks - only if you're in React
'@hey-api/client-axios' // In case you are looking for axios! A lot of people usually are!
// Check for othe supported plugins too!
],
});
One command later, npx @hey-api/openapi-ts — src/client/ holds your types, a typed SDK, Zod schemas, and (in a React app or Vue app) query hooks or composables. Same spec in, same code out, every time. That determinism is what changes things: the generated client stops being something a person maintains and becomes something the spec produces.

What it feels like to use
The shift is bigger than the keystrokes saved, and the interesting part is the boundary, because validation runs in both directions there, depending on whether data is coming in or going out.
Reading a post is a GET: the data is something the backend produced, so I validate what comes in.
import { getPostById } from './client/sdk.gen';
import { zPost } from './client/zod.gen';const { data } = await getPostById({ path: { id } });
const post = zPost.parse(data); // the response throws if the backend lied
// `post` is guaranteed to match the spec: title, body, publishedAt and all
Validating GET responses however might be a double work considering, it is the job of backend to adhere to the schemas it publishes/provides, and if you find yourself in strict need for this, I would suggest you look into more data contract restrictive API development like GraphQL (Couldn’t find a clear way to mention the discipline!).
Creating a post is a POST: now the data is something I produced, so I validate what goes out, before it ever leaves the client.
import { createPost } from './client/sdk.gen';
import { zCreatePost } from './client/zod.gen';const body = zCreatePost.parse({ title, body: draft }); // the request catches bad input early
const { data } = await createPost({ body });
So, to answer the question the first snippet always raises: it depends on the direction. On a GET you mostly validate the response you fetched; on a POST or PUT you also get a schema for the request, so you can check the payload before sending it. (Notice the create schema doesn't include id or publishedAt, those are the server's to set, and the spec says so, so the generated request schema leaves them out.) Hey API produces a Zod schema for each side because the spec describes both; the names just mirror your operations and types.
Zod is the part I’d never give back
Types are a compile-time promise. They evaporate at runtime, and the backend doesn’t read your .ts files, it can rename a field, send a null, or ship a half-finished deploy, and TypeScript will happily wave it through. Zod is what actually stands at the door, in either direction.
What sold me is that Hey API generates Zod schemas from the spec’s formats, not just its shapes. Remember those uuid and date-time markers on the Post? They come out as z.string().uuid() and z.string().datetime() — a schema that's stricter and more honest than the one I'd hand-write on a busy afternoon. So a .parse() call doesn't merely check that fields exist; it checks they mean what the contract says. When something drifts, a malformed response coming in, or a bad payload about to go out, it fails loudly at the edge instead of silently corrupting state three components deep. That single capability validation generated from the same source as the client is the thing I'd been missing for years.

And if you’re in React/Vue, the hooks/composables come free
One more plugin worth calling out, with a caveat: @tanstack/react-query only makes sense in React-based environments. If that's you, Hey API generates the query and mutation options straight from the spec, so the useQuery/useMutation wrappers and query keys I used to hand-write around swagger-typescript-api simply aren't my job anymore. I spread the generated options into a hook and move on. If you're not in React, you leave this plugin off and the SDK-plus-Zod combination stands on its own.
If you are into Vue, you can also check the plugin @pinia/colada, compatible that the data fetching layer for Vue.

What this changes; development, architecture, and workflow
It’s tempting to file this under “nice productivity tool,” but the real shift is structural, and it lands in three places at once.
Architecture. The contract used to live in several hand-maintained files; Types here, fetchers there, hooks somewhere else with nothing enforcing agreement between them. Now there’s one generated src/client/ that nobody edits by hand, with the spec sitting above it as the single source. TypeScript guards the shape at compile time, Zod guards it at runtime: two independent checks, both derived from the same document.
Development. Adding a feature used to mean four files; A type, a fetcher, a hook, then the component. Now I write the component and reach for an already-generated SDK call or hook. The boilerplate I used to review in PRs isn’t authored anymore, so the diffs are smaller and the reviews are about behavior, not plumbing.
Workflow. This is the part that quietly fixes team friction. When the backend renames body, they update the spec; I regenerate and TypeScript instantly lights up every spot that needs to change; No grep, no guessing, no release-freeze archaeology. Onboarding gets blunt, too: "where's the Post type?" now has exactly one answer regenerate and look in src/client. The spec becomes the negotiation, versioned and reviewed like any other code, instead of a Slack thread nobody can find next quarter.

Make it automatic
The discipline only holds if regeneration isn’t a step someone has to remember. I keep it in CI: regenerate the client, then fail the build if the committed output changed.
- run: npx openapi-ts
- run: git diff --exit-code src/client/ # spec changed but nobody regeneratedWhether you commit the generated client (easier diffs, works offline) or gitignore it and regenerate fresh is a matter of team taste. Either way, you trade “I hope someone updated the types” for a guarantee.

Going further: version the spec and the SDK as packages
The single-repo version of this is great until the team grows past one repo. Once the backend and frontend live apart, or you have several frontends consuming the same API, pointing every project at a raw openapi.json URL starts to feel fragile. There's no notion of which version of the contract a given app was built against, and a backend deploy can silently shift the spec under everyone
